metadata.title: Winner-Takes-All: AI Capability Loop Dynamics
metadata.description: A causal loop diagram of the reinforcing and balancing forces that determine whether the AI industry tips toward a single dominant player or remains an oligopoly with leapfrogging.
metadata.author: Tracking AI Course

nodes.label: Model Capability
nodes.description: Aggregate quality of a frontier model: reasoning, code generation, agentic task completion, evaluation scores.

nodes.label: Code Generation Quality
nodes.description: How reliably the model produces correct, maintainable code for real engineering tasks.

nodes.label: R&D Productivity
nodes.description: How fast the AI lab itself can ship experiments, training infrastructure, and next-gen models.

nodes.label: Autonomous Research
nodes.description: The model's ability to design, run, and interpret its own ML experiments without a human in the loop.

nodes.label: Architecture Discovery Rate
nodes.description: Rate at which novel model architectures, training recipes, and optimizations are found.

nodes.label: Market Share
nodes.description: Share of paid frontier-model usage and enterprise contracts.

nodes.label: Capital
nodes.description: Cash on hand from revenue and strategic investment available to spend on training.

nodes.label: Compute Scale
nodes.description: Total training compute available to the lab — GPUs, datacenters, electricity contracts.

nodes.label: User Adoption
nodes.description: Number of active users, agents, and enterprise integrations running on the model.

nodes.label: Proprietary Interaction Data
nodes.description: Private signal from real-world usage: traces, preferences, error corrections — not available to competitors.

nodes.label: Compute Demand
nodes.description: Compute the lab needs to train and serve increasingly large models.

nodes.label: Compute Scarcity / Cost
nodes.description: Market price and queue depth for advanced GPUs and power capacity.

nodes.label: Model Complexity
nodes.description: Internal complexity that makes the system harder to evaluate against ground truth.

nodes.label: Evaluation Difficulty
nodes.description: How hard it is to know whether a new model is actually better, beyond benchmark gaming.

nodes.label: Visibility / Reverse Engineering
nodes.description: How much of a leader's breakthrough leaks via papers, employee mobility, prompts, and inference traces.

nodes.label: Competitor Adoption
nodes.description: How quickly rival labs implement and ship the leader's techniques.

nodes.label: Inference Cost
nodes.description: Per-token cost to run the model in production.

edges.description: Better models generate better code.
Better code generation accelerates the lab's own engineering.
Faster R&D produces stronger next-generation models.
More capable models can run more of their own research.
Autonomous experimentation finds new architectures faster.
New architectures raise capability.
Better models win more customers.
Market share converts to revenue and unlocks strategic investment.
Capital buys GPUs, power, and datacenters.
More training compute lifts model capability.
Better models attract more users and agents.
Real usage produces unique training signal.
Proprietary data feeds back into training and finetuning.
Pushing capability requires more compute per training run.
Higher demand raises the price and queue depth for compute.
Scarce, expensive compute slows training throughput and improvement.
More capable models are also more complex internally.
Complexity makes ground-truth evaluation harder.
Hard evaluation introduces noise and slows reliable progress.
Visible capability invites reverse engineering and talent flow.
What's visible gets copied.
Fast-following erodes the leader's relative advantage.
Bigger, better models cost more per token to serve.
Higher inference cost suppresses adoption — the 'best' model is not always the most used.

loops.label: R1: Recursive Self-Improvement
loops.description: Better code generation makes the lab itself more productive, which produces stronger next-gen models. The core 'winner-takes-all' hypothesis.

loops.label: R2: Autonomous Research (Supercritical)
loops.description: If the model can run its own experiments and discover its own architectures, the loop closes inside the company and decouples from human R&D pace.

loops.label: R3: Capital → Compute → Capability
loops.description: Capability wins market share, market share unlocks capital, capital buys compute, compute lifts capability. This is where Google's $40B Anthropic investment plugs in.

loops.label: R4: Data Flywheel
loops.description: Adoption produces proprietary interaction data competitors can't see. This is the lock-in mechanism most likely to tip the system to a single winner.

loops.label: B1: Compute Constraint
loops.description: Pushing capability raises compute demand, which raises scarcity and cost, which throttles everyone — including the leader.

loops.label: B2: Evaluation Bottleneck
loops.description: Capability raises complexity, complexity makes evaluation harder, and harder evaluation slows the rate of *reliable* progress.

loops.label: B3: Diffusion / Fast-Follow
loops.description: Visible capability invites reverse engineering and talent mobility. Competitor adoption erodes the leader's relative advantage even when AI writes the code.

loops.label: B4: Cost-Performance Friction
loops.description: Frontier models are expensive to serve. High inference cost suppresses adoption, which weakens the data flywheel.
